exercises.title: Rolling update vs blue-green vs canary — pick the strategy
exercises.instructions:
For each change, pick the correct deployment strategy:
A: Rename a database column (backward-incompatible migration)
B: Update recommendation algorithm (same API)
C: Change API response format (breaking change)
D: Upgrade log format from text to JSON
E: A/B test new checkout UI with 10% of users
exercises.expected_solution:
A: Rename DB column → BLUE-GREEN
   Rolling update: v1 and v2 run simultaneously → v1 reads old column name → breaks!
   Blue-green: run migration (add new column, copy data) → switch to v2 → drop old column

B: Recommendation algorithm update → CANARY (or rolling update)
   API unchanged, just algorithm different → safe to expose to 5% first
   Monitor: recommendation CTR, page load time, error rate

C: API response format change → BLUE-GREEN
   Clients v1 (old format) and v2 (new format) are incompatible
   Blue-green: coordinate with client teams → switch all at once
   Or: versioned API (/v2/recommendations) → gradual client migration

D: Log format update → ROLLING UPDATE
   Internal change, no external impact, low risk
   Rolling update is perfectly safe here

E: New checkout UI → CANARY (or feature flag)
   10% users see new UI, 90% see old
   Monitor: conversion rate, error rate, session duration
   Can be done at app layer (feature flag) or k8s layer (canary weight)

test_cases.expected:
A: Rename DB column → BLUE-GREEN
   Rolling update: v1 and v2 run simultaneously → v1 reads old column name → breaks!
   Blue-green: run migration (add new column, copy data) → switch to v2 → drop old column

B: Recommendation algorithm update → CANARY (or rolling update)
   API unchanged, just algorithm different → safe to expose to 5% first
   Monitor: recommendation CTR, page load time, error rate

C: API response format change → BLUE-GREEN
   Clients v1 (old format) and v2 (new format) are incompatible
   Blue-green: coordinate with client teams → switch all at once
   Or: versioned API (/v2/recommendations) → gradual client migration

D: Log format update → ROLLING UPDATE
   Internal change, no external impact, low risk
   Rolling update is perfectly safe here

E: New checkout UI → CANARY (or feature flag)
   10% users see new UI, 90% see old
   Monitor: conversion rate, error rate, session duration
   Can be done at app layer (feature flag) or k8s layer (canary weight)

hints: Blue-green: breaking changes, DB migrations
Canary: risk assessment, progressive exposure
Rolling update: default for low-risk, backward-compatible changes

exercises.title: Complete the blue-green Service selector switch
exercises.instructions: Fill in the kubectl patch to switch from blue to green.

blanks.hint: kubectl subcommand to update a specific field

hints: kubectl patch: update specific JSON/YAML path in a resource
Service selector switch is atomic — instant, zero-downtime
Rollback: set version back to 'blue'

exercises.title: Write blue-green Deployment and Service YAMLs
exercises.instructions:
Write blue and green Deployments + Service for Razorpay payment service:
1. payment-blue: 3 replicas, image: payment-service:v1.0, labels: {app: payment, version: blue}
2. payment-green: 3 replicas, image: payment-service:v2.0, same structure
3. Service selector: version: blue (points to current active)
exercises.expected_solution:
# k8s/payment-blue.yml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata: { name: payment-blue, namespace: payments }
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels: { app: payment, version: blue }
  template:
    metadata:
      labels: { app: payment, version: blue }
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: payment
        image: razorpay/payment-service:v1.0
        ports: [{ containerPort: 8080 }]
        resources:
          requests: { memory: 512Mi, cpu: 250m }
          limits: { memory: 768Mi, cpu: 500m }
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet: { path: /actuator/health/readiness, port: 8080 }
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
---
# k8s/payment-green.yml (identical structure, version: green, image: v2.0)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata: { name: payment-green, namespace: payments }
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels: { app: payment, version: green }
  template:
    metadata:
      labels: { app: payment, version: green }
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: payment
        image: razorpay/payment-service:v2.0
        ports: [{ containerPort: 8080 }]
---
# k8s/payment-service.yml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata: { name: payment-service, namespace: payments }
spec:
  selector:
    app: payment
    version: blue    # ← only routes to blue pods
  ports: [{ port: 80, targetPort: 8080 }]
  type: ClusterIP
hints: Both Deployments run simultaneously: 6 total pods (3 blue + 3 green)
Service selector: must match BOTH app AND version labels
Rollback: patch service back to version: blue (instant)

exercises.title: Canary Ingress with Nginx annotations
exercises.instructions:
Write stable + canary Ingress YAMLs for Zerodha order service:
1. Stable Ingress: /api/orders → order-stable Service
2. Canary Ingress: 5% traffic weight annotation
3. Show: how to increase weight from 5% to 25%
exercises.expected_solution:
# k8s/order-stable-ingress.yml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: order-stable
  namespace: trading
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/ingress.class: nginx
spec:
  rules:
  - http:
      paths:
      - path: /api/orders
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: order-stable
            port: { number: 80 }
---
# k8s/order-canary-ingress.yml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: order-canary
  namespace: trading
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/ingress.class: nginx
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary: 'true'
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-weight: '5'  # 5% of traffic
spec:
  rules:
  - http:
      paths:
      - path: /api/orders
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: order-canary
            port: { number: 80 }

# Increase canary weight to 25%:
# kubectl patch ingress order-canary -n trading \
#   --type=merge -p '{"metadata":{"annotations":{"nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-weight":"25"}}}'
hints: canary: 'true': tells nginx this is the canary ingress
canary-weight: percentage of traffic to send here
Weight increase: kubectl patch to update annotation value

exercises.title: Blue-green switch shell script
exercises.instructions:
Write a shell script for blue-green deployment:
1. Detect current active (blue or green)
2. Deploy new version to inactive side
3. Run smoke test against inactive side
4. Switch Service on success, rollback on failure
5. Option to keep old side running or scale down
exercises.expected_solution:
#!/bin/bash
# blue-green-deploy.sh
set -e

NAMESPACE='payments'
IMAGE=$1  # e.g., razorpay/payment-service:v2.0

# 1. Detect active version:
CURRENT=$(kubectl get service payment-service -n $NAMESPACE \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.selector.version}')
if [ '$CURRENT' = 'blue' ]; then
  INACTIVE='green'; ACTIVE='blue'
else
  INACTIVE='blue'; ACTIVE='green'
fi

echo "Current active: $ACTIVE. Deploying to: $INACTIVE"

# 2. Update inactive deployment image:
kubectl set image deployment/payment-$INACTIVE \
  payment=$IMAGE -n $NAMESPACE

# 3. Wait for inactive to be ready:
kubectl rollout status deployment/payment-$INACTIVE -n $NAMESPACE --timeout=5m

# 4. Smoke test against inactive pods:
INACTIVE_POD=$(kubectl get pods -n $NAMESPACE -l version=$INACTIVE \
  -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')
if ! kubectl exec $INACTIVE_POD -n $NAMESPACE -- \
     wget -qO- http://localhost:8080/actuator/health | grep '"status":"UP"'; then
  echo 'Smoke test FAILED — not switching'
  exit 1
fi

# 5. Switch Service:
kubectl patch service payment-service -n $NAMESPACE \
  -p "{\"spec\":{\"selector\":{\"version\":\"$INACTIVE\"}}}"
echo "Switched! Now active: $INACTIVE"

# 6. Scale down old version (optional, keeps it for rollback):
# kubectl scale deployment/payment-$ACTIVE --replicas=1 -n $NAMESPACE
hints: jsonpath: extract specific field from kubectl output
Smoke test before switch: validate new version is healthy
Scale to 1 (not 0): keep old version for instant rollback without needing to rebuild

exercises.title: Canary success metrics and automated rollback
exercises.instructions:
Write PromQL queries to compare canary vs stable:
1. Error rate by version label
2. P99 latency by version
3. Prometheus alert rule: auto-rollback if canary error rate > 2× stable
4. Show: how Grafana dashboard would show canary vs stable
exercises.expected_solution:
# Canary pods have label: version=canary
# Stable pods have label: version=stable
# (Set via Deployment labels → propagated to pod labels → Micrometer global tag)

# 1. Error rate by version:
# sum(rate(payments_total_total{status='failure'}[5m])) by (version)
# / sum(rate(payments_total_total[5m])) by (version) * 100
# Shows: stable=0.1%, canary=5.2% → red flag!

# 2. P99 latency by version:
# histogram_quantile(0.99,
#   sum(rate(payments_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, version)
# )
# Shows: stable=180ms, canary=850ms → performance regression!

# 3. Auto-rollback alert rule:
prometheus_alert_rule = '''
- alert: CanaryHighErrorRate
  expr: |
    (
      sum(rate(payments_total_total{status='failure', version='canary'}[5m]))
      / sum(rate(payments_total_total{version='canary'}[5m]))
    )
    >
    (
      sum(rate(payments_total_total{status='failure', version='stable'}[5m]))
      / sum(rate(payments_total_total{version='stable'}[5m]))
    ) * 2
  for: 5m
  annotations:
    summary: 'Canary error rate is 2x stable — rollback immediately'
    runbook_url: 'https://wiki.razorpay.com/runbooks/canary-rollback'
'''

# 4. Grafana dashboard:
# Panel 1: Error rate time series, colored lines: blue=stable, orange=canary
# Panel 2: P99 latency, same colors
# Panel 3: Traffic split (5%, 25%, 50%, 100% progression)
# Panel 4: Rollback button (Grafana callback or PagerDuty integration)

public class CanaryMonitoring { public static void main(String[] a) { System.out.println("Canary metrics: error rate + p99 by version label"); } }

expected_stdout_contains: Canary metrics: error rate + p99 by version label

hints: version label on pods: Micrometer global tag propagates to all metrics
2× stable error rate: reasonable rollback threshold
For automation: AlertManager webhook → script that patches ingress weight to 0

exercises.title: Feature flags for application-layer canary
exercises.instructions:
Implement feature flags as an alternative to k8s-level canary:
1. @FeatureFlag annotation or Spring @ConditionalOnProperty
2. Flipt/LaunchDarkly concept — evaluate flag per user
3. Gradual rollout: 10% of users get new feature
4. @SpringBootTest: verify flag-based routing
5. Compare: k8s canary vs application-level feature flags
hints: userId.hashCode() % 100: stable bucket assignment (same user always in same bucket)
rollout-percentage in ConfigMap: update from 10% to 100% without redeployment
Feature flags vs k8s canary: flags work at user level, canary at request level

exercises.title: Argo Rollouts — automated canary analysis
exercises.instructions:
Write an Argo Rollout with automated analysis:
1. Rollout with canarySteps: 5% → pause 30min → 25% → pause 1h → 100%
2. AnalysisTemplate: check error rate from Prometheus
3. Auto-rollback if analysis fails
4. Show: how Argo Rollouts differs from manual canary
exercises.expected_solution:
# k8s/argo-rollout.yml (requires Argo Rollouts installed)
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Rollout
metadata:
  name: payment-rollout
  namespace: payments
spec:
  replicas: 10
  selector:
    matchLabels: { app: payment }
  template:
    metadata:
      labels: { app: payment }
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: payment
        image: razorpay/payment-service:v2.0
  strategy:
    canary:
      steps:
      - setWeight: 5          # 5% canary traffic
      - analysis:             # run analysis before proceeding
          templates:
          - templateName: payment-error-rate
      - pause: { duration: 30m }  # wait 30 min
      - setWeight: 25
      - analysis:
          templates:
          - templateName: payment-error-rate
      - pause: { duration: 1h }
      - setWeight: 100        # full rollout
---
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: AnalysisTemplate
metadata:
  name: payment-error-rate
  namespace: payments
spec:
  metrics:
  - name: error-rate
    interval: 5m
    successCondition: result[0] < 0.02   # error rate < 2%
    failureLimit: 2   # fail if condition not met twice
    provider:
      prometheus:
        address: http://prometheus:9090
        query: |
          sum(rate(payments_total_total{status='failure'}[5m]))
          / sum(rate(payments_total_total[5m]))

# Auto-rollback: if analysis fails → setWeight to 0 → rollback to previous version
# Manual override: kubectl argo rollouts abort payment-rollout (instant 0%)

# kubectl argo rollouts get rollout payment-rollout --watch
hints: Argo Rollouts: extends k8s Deployment with progressive delivery
analysis: automated Prometheus check between each weight step
failureLimit: 2 retries before auto-rollback

exercises.title: Dark launch for payment gateway change
exercises.instructions:
Implement dark launch for a new payment gateway:
1. Route 0% of real traffic to new gateway (canary-weight=0)
2. Use canary-by-header: X-Dark-Launch: true for test requests
3. Load test against dark launch pod
4. Show: Nginx Ingress header-based routing
5. When to promote from dark to 5% real traffic
exercises.expected_solution:
# Dark launch: canary-weight=0, but header routes to it

# Stable Ingress (all real traffic):
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: payment-stable
  namespace: payments
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/ingress.class: nginx
spec:
  rules:
  - http:
      paths:
      - path: /api/payments
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service: { name: payment-stable, port: { number: 80 } }
---
# Dark launch Ingress (0% random, but test header routes here):
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: payment-dark
  namespace: payments
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary: 'true'
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-weight: '0'           # 0% real traffic
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-by-header: 'X-Dark-Launch'
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-by-header-value: 'true'
spec:
  rules:
  - http:
      paths:
      - path: /api/payments
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service: { name: payment-dark, port: { number: 80 } }

# Test with dark launch:
# curl -H 'X-Dark-Launch: true' https://api.razorpay.com/api/payments
# → always routes to dark launch pod (not real users)

# PROMOTE TO REAL CANARY:
# After: load test passes, monitoring looks good, no errors in dark launch
# kubectl patch ingress payment-dark \
#   --type=merge -p '{"metadata":{"annotations":{"nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-weight":"5"}}}'
hints: canary-weight: 0 + header routing: only test traffic reaches dark launch
Header-based routing: QA team sends X-Dark-Launch: true in their tests
Dark launch to canary: only promote when confident in correctness

exercises.title: Blue-green rollback timing comparison
exercises.instructions:
Compare rollback timing for three strategies:
1. Rolling update rollback (kubectl rollout undo)
2. Blue-green rollback (kubectl patch service)
3. Canary rollback (patch ingress weight to 0)

For each: how long does rollback take? How many users affected?
exercises.expected_solution:
1. ROLLING UPDATE ROLLBACK (kubectl rollout undo):
   k8s terminates new pods one at a time, starts old ones
   10 replicas: each pod takes 30-60s → total rollback time: ~5-10 minutes
   Users affected: during rollback, some pods are old (v1) and some new (v2)
   At worst: 100% of requests hit v2 (broken) until rollback completes
   Use when: low-risk change, single environment

2. BLUE-GREEN ROLLBACK (kubectl patch service):
   Service selector change is atomic (iptables rules update in <1 second)
   Total rollback time: ~1-2 SECONDS
   Users affected: requests in-flight when switch happens (~50-100 requests)
   Old blue pods: still running, immediately receive all traffic again
   Use when: major releases, DB migrations (old version compatible with old schema)

3. CANARY ROLLBACK (patch ingress weight to 0):
   Set canary-weight from 5% to 0 → Nginx stops routing to canary pods
   Total rollback time: ~5-10 seconds (Nginx config reload)
   Users affected at 5% canary: only 5% were seeing broken code
   At 5%: at most 5% of requests hit broken code before rollback
   Use when: progressive rollout, risk-assessed changes

SUMMARY:
  Fastest rollback: blue-green (1-2 seconds)
  Least users affected: canary (only % that were routed to it)
  Default choice: rolling update (simplest)
  High-risk: canary with low initial weight (minimize exposure)
  Breaking changes: blue-green (predictable cutover)

test_cases.expected:
1. ROLLING UPDATE ROLLBACK (kubectl rollout undo):
   k8s terminates new pods one at a time, starts old ones
   10 replicas: each pod takes 30-60s → total rollback time: ~5-10 minutes
   Users affected: during rollback, some pods are old (v1) and some new (v2)
   At worst: 100% of requests hit v2 (broken) until rollback completes
   Use when: low-risk change, single environment

2. BLUE-GREEN ROLLBACK (kubectl patch service):
   Service selector change is atomic (iptables rules update in <1 second)
   Total rollback time: ~1-2 SECONDS
   Users affected: requests in-flight when switch happens (~50-100 requests)
   Old blue pods: still running, immediately receive all traffic again
   Use when: major releases, DB migrations (old version compatible with old schema)

3. CANARY ROLLBACK (patch ingress weight to 0):
   Set canary-weight from 5% to 0 → Nginx stops routing to canary pods
   Total rollback time: ~5-10 seconds (Nginx config reload)
   Users affected at 5% canary: only 5% were seeing broken code
   At 5%: at most 5% of requests hit broken code before rollback
   Use when: progressive rollout, risk-assessed changes

SUMMARY:
  Fastest rollback: blue-green (1-2 seconds)
  Least users affected: canary (only % that were routed to it)
  Default choice: rolling update (simplest)
  High-risk: canary with low initial weight (minimize exposure)
  Breaking changes: blue-green (predictable cutover)

hints: Service selector switch: atomic, ~1 second
Canary rollback: limits blast radius to canary %
Rolling update rollback: same time as rolling update (~5-10 min for 10 pods)

exercises.title: Complete Razorpay blue-green with smoke tests
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Razorpay blue-green deployment:
1. Two Deployments (blue/green) with readiness probes
2. Service with version selector
3. Smoke test: @SpringBootTest verifying health + payment endpoint
4. Shell script: deploy → smoke test → switch
5. All @SpringBootTest tests pass
hints: Smoke tests run BEFORE switch: validate green before exposing to users
Same tests in blue and green: ensures new version is compatible
In CI: run these tests against inactive deployment via port-forward

exercises.title: Progressive canary rollout with monitoring
exercises.instructions:
Write the progressive canary deployment procedure:
1. Start: 5% canary weight
2. After 30 min: check error rate and p99
3. If healthy: increase to 25%
4. After 1 hour: increase to 50%
5. After 2 hours: 100% (retire stable)
6. Show: the kubectl commands for each step + monitoring checks
exercises.expected_solution:
#!/bin/bash
# progressive-canary.sh
set -e

NAMESPACE='payments'
WEIGHTS=(5 25 50 100)
WAIT_MINUTES=(30 60 120 0)  # wait after each step

for i in 0 1 2 3; do
  WEIGHT=${WEIGHTS[$i]}
  WAIT=${WAIT_MINUTES[$i]}
  
  echo "=== Setting canary weight: ${WEIGHT}% ==="
  kubectl patch ingress payment-canary -n $NAMESPACE \
    --type=merge -p \
    "{\"metadata\":{\"annotations\":{\"nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-weight\":\"$WEIGHT\"}}}"
  
  if [ $WEIGHT -eq 100 ]; then
    echo "100%: canary is now primary. Retiring stable..."
    kubectl scale deployment payment-stable --replicas=0 -n $NAMESPACE
    break
  fi
  
  echo "Waiting ${WAIT} minutes before next step..."
  sleep ${WAIT}m
  
  # Monitor: check Prometheus metrics
  echo 'Checking canary error rate...'
  # In production: query Prometheus API:
  # ERROR_RATE=$(curl -s 'http://prometheus:9090/api/v1/query' \
  #   --data-urlencode 'query=sum(rate(payments_total{status=failure,version=canary}[5m]))/sum(rate(payments_total{version=canary}[5m]))' \
  #   | jq -r '.data.result[0].value[1]')
  # if (( $(echo "$ERROR_RATE > 0.02" | bc -l) )); then
  #   echo 'ERROR RATE TOO HIGH — ROLLING BACK'
  #   kubectl patch ingress payment-canary --type=merge -p '{"metadata":{"annotations":{"nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-weight":"0"}}}'
  #   exit 1
  # fi
  echo 'Monitoring check passed — proceeding to next step'
done

echo '=== Canary rollout COMPLETE ==='
hints: Progressive weights: 5 → 25 → 50 → 100 with monitoring between steps
Scale stable to 0: after 100% on canary (saves resources)
Prometheus API: query programmatically to automate rollback decision

exercises.title: DB-compatible blue-green with Flyway migration
exercises.instructions:
Design a DB-compatible blue-green deployment:
1. Phase 1: Deploy migration (add new_column, keep old_column)
2. Phase 2: Deploy green (reads both columns for compatibility)
3. Phase 3: Switch to green
4. Phase 4: Cleanup migration (drop old_column)
5. Show: expand-contract pattern
hints: Expand: add new column, keep old (backward compatible)
Contract: drop old column only after all code reads new column
Dual-read: v2 reads new column, falls back to old during transition

exercises.title: Automatic canary rollback on SLO breach
exercises.instructions:
Integrate canary with SLO monitoring:
1. PrometheusClient to query current canary error rate
2. If error rate > SLO budget × 2: trigger rollback via kubectl
3. @Scheduled: check every 5 minutes during active canary
4. Log: canary metrics and rollback decision
5. @SpringBootTest: verify logic
hints: 2× SLO budget: aggressive rollback to protect error budget
@Scheduled monitoring: automated without human intervention
Prometheus HTTP API: /api/v1/query with PromQL for error rate

exercises.title: Complete M21 deployment synthesis — all 5 topics
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete Razorpay production deployment stack demonstrating all Module 21 concepts:
1. Dockerfile health check config (T21.1)
2. K8s readiness + liveness probes (T21.2)
3. Config values via @Value (T21.3)
4. CI validation (T21.4)
5. Canary feature flag (T21.5)
6. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): all subsystems verified

All tests pass — this is the Module 21 complete synthesis.
hints: 3 tests: one per core concern (health, config, metrics)
All 5 M21 topics represented: Dockerfile (config), k8s probes (health), ConfigMap (@Value), CI (tests pass), canary (feature flag)
Module 21 complete: production-grade deployment stack from Dockerfile to progressive delivery
